content.full_text:
Governing Board

Bernardo F. Armas Sr., President
Juan P. Leyva, Clerk
Norma Estrella, Member
Ernestine Jones, Member
B. Toby Mange, Member

Dr. Nancy A. Montalvo
Superintendent

November 15, 1990


Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, California  95814

RE:  Request for Opinion

Dear Executive Director:

     Please accept this letter as a request for a written opinion.

     This request for a written opinion concerns an unresolved controversy in the District which began on May 29, 1990, and continues to the present. On May 29, 1990, Board President Bernardo F. Armas, Sr., directed that a written message be prepared, copied and delivered to all students in the District.  A copy of that written message is the first enclosure with this letter.  The written message also was translated into Spanish and delivered to students in the District.  A copy of the translated message is the second enclosure with this letter.  There are six schools in the District and approximately 3,700 students.  Two of the Board members objected to the written message, but it was processed at the direction of Mr. Armas.

     After the written message from Mr. Armas was delivered to all students in the District, the then current Interim Superintendent asked the attorneys for the District for a legal opinion.  Attorneys for the District advised the Interim Superintendent orally and later in writing that the use of public funds on May 29, 1990, violated Government Code section 89001 (as amended by Proposition 73 in 1988) and regulations adopted by the Fair Political Practices Commission.


4350 Otay Mesa Road, San Ysidro, CA  92073-1685  (619) 428-4476

Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 15, 1990
Page 2


     On June 5, 1990, Mr. Armas delivered a personal check to the
District for $232.73 which reimbursed the District for the cost
of duplicating his written message. Shortly thereafter the Grand
Jury in San Diego County announced it was investigating the mat-
ter and other issues involving the District. The third enclosure
with this letter is a copy of a newspaper article on the con-
troversy dated June 14, 1990.

     On July 1, 1990, I started my employment with the District
as the Superintendent. On October 16, 1990, the Grand Jury
issued a report noting on page 3 in paragraph eight that the Dis-
trict Attorney found no criminal conduct involving newsletters in
1990 and earlier years. A copy of that report is the fourth
enclosure with this letter.

     On October 19, 1990, I sent Mr. Armas a letter requesting an
additional $153.00, because the costs of his written message in-
cluded more than copying costs. A copy of that letter is the
fifth enclosure with this letter.

     On October 24, 1990, Mr. Armas sent me a letter requesting a
refund of the $232.73. A copy of his letter is the sixth and
final enclosure with this letter. Mr. Armas now argues that the
report of the Grand Jury "rule in his favor" and that Proposition
73 was repealed.

     What should I do? The attorneys for the District have
not changed their legal advice. Should Mr. Armas be given a
refund or should the District continue to seek reimbursement for
all of the costs associated with the written message of May 29,
1990?

     Thank you for your assistance in this matter. If you need
any additional information, please contact me. A speedy written
opinion will be most appreciated.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Nancy Montalvo, Ed.D.
                                        Superintendent

NAM/ca

cc:  Mr. Bernardo F. Armas, Sr.
     Mr. Richard J. Currier, Esq.

San Ysidro
School District
Excellence is our goal

Governing Board

Bernardo F. Armas Sr., President
Juan P. Leyva, Clerk
Norma Estrella, Member
Ernestine Jones, Member
B. Toby Mange, Member

Dr. Nancy A. Montalvo
Superintendent

October 19, 1990

MR. Bernardo Armas, Sr.
2551 East Beyer Blvd.
San Ysidro, CA 92073

Dear Mr. Armas:

Please note the list below which includes the estimated expenses incurred by the district for the publication of the "President's Message".

Name                          Time              Mileage         Total

Sir Speedy                                                        $232.73

Christine Alonso              3.0 hrs.                            67.53

Oscar Villarreal              3.0 hrs.                            30.08

Anita Lopez                   1.0 hr.            25.3             10.62
                                                                  6.58

Matilde Rosa                  .50 hr.            1.9              5.54
                                                                  .49

Carmen Ortiz                  .50 hr.                             5.54

Antonio Cervantes             .50 hr.            2.5              6.07
                                                                  .65

School Clerks                 1.75 hrs.                           19.90

                                                                  $383.73
                              Less personal check (232.73)

                                                                  $153.00

4350 Otay Mesa Road, San Ysidro, CA 92073-1685 (619) 428-4476

Mr. Bernardo Armas, Sr.
October 19, 1990
Page 2

Please remit payment of $153.00 to our Business Office at
your earliest convenience.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Nancy Montalvo, Ed.D.
Superintendent

NAM/ca
cc: Ken Clark, Business Manager


San Ysidro
School District
Excellence is our goal

May 29, 1990

SAN YSIDRO SCHOOL DISTRICT

A message
from the
Board President

My promise to you when I asked for your vote 17 months ago was
leadership that brings results. Improved school facilities,
greater accountability and more responsive to parents.

I would like to point out the progress we have made as well as
areas where more progress needs to be made!

LEADERSHIP THAT BRINGS RESULTS

There is a strong and active direction in the District since I
became a Member of your Governing Board. We are willing to ask
the tough questions about test scores, school safety, student dropout,
divisions existing and interdistirct Student Transfers. We are
classroom discipline and interdistrict Student Transfers. We are
also willing to make people more accountable and raise their
level of commitment so that together we are finding answers.

IMPROVED SCHOOL FACILITIES

Most of our schools are at or beyond capacity.

We are talking with City and the Otay Mesa Developers about additional facilities. We are also looking at the beautification and
cleanliness of all campuses. Perhaps by next Spring we will see
some flowers and greenery around our schools. We need to take
more pride in our school facilities.

GREATER ACCOUNTABILITY

Following Board and parent concerns Administrators and Teachers
are now being more critically evaluated and goals and objectives
are now being more critically evaluated and goals and objectives
are being set. MVASP test results are not yet available, but they
are being set. MVASP test results are not yet available, but they
are being set. Indication of accountability and commitment on the
will be some indication of accountability and commitment on the
part of those who lead and teach in the District.

MORE RESPONSIVE TO PARENTS

The Board and District are now more responsive to parents. We
listen to your concerns, invite you to be members of committees
to solve problems, initiated a Suggestion Box at every school,
to solve problems, initiated a Suggestion Box at every school,
hold parent meetings for questions and answers and are genuinely
open to parent opinions.

I thank all of the parents who rode the Bus to City Hall to help
us acquire the Community Development Block Grant Funds.

Conclusion, more forceful action and greater results will be possible following the appointment of the permanent Superintendent.
This Board will take action in late June. This District is ready
This Board will take action in late June. This District is ready
for improvement with commitment and attention to the smooth running
of an effective educational community.

[signature]
Bernard F. Armas, Sr.
Board President

San Ysidro
School District
Excellence is our goal

29 de Mayo de 1990                    DISTRITO ESCOLAR DE SAN YSIDRO


Un Mensaje del Presidente
de la Mesa Directiva

Mi promese hecha a ustedes hace 17 meses, cuando los Rods su-
frieron con lagunas en que uo rse producze resultados, mejores inter-
venciones escolares, mayor responsabilidad y mayor atención en
los padres.

Yo desearte hacer notar el progreso que hemos logrado así como
las áreas en que de rogujerce mayor atención:

LIDERAZGO QUE PRODUZCA RESULTADOS

Desde que me convertí en miembro de Mesa Directiva existe
que el Distrito una vigorosa y activa dirección. Estamos dis-
puesto a Distrito una vigorosa y activa dirección. Estamos dis-
puesto apocar preguntas sobre el desempeño de estudiantes, entendaza en el
rola de la escuela, descripción de estudiantes entre distritos,
según de clases y transferencias. Ahora estamos dispuestos a hacer a la gente más responsable
Además estamos disponibles a hacer a la gente más responsable
y alaviar su nivel de compromiso así que juntos estamos encon-
trando respuestas.

MEJORES INSTALACIONES ESCOLARES

La mayor parte de nuestras escuelas están a toda su capacidad
o excedidas.

Prendemos en pláticas con la Ciudad y los urbanizadores de Otay
Mesa y como acerca estamos procuran-
mente sobre instalaciones adicionales. Para la próxima Primavera solicitaremos encuepleceremos
el aire para la próxima Primavera solicitaremos encuepleceremos
director de nuestras escuelas.
más de nuestras instalaciones escolares.

MAYOR RESPONSABILIDAD

Debido al interés de la Mesa y de los padres, los administra-
dores y los maestros están siendo evaluados cuidadosamente. Hemos hecho varios cambios y objetivos. Algunos resultados de
pruebas nos han indicado los cambios han sido efectivos. Un invitación de
pruebas no están aún disponibles, pero serán un invitación de
pruebas no está aún disponibles, pero serán un invitación por parte de aquellos que di-
la responsabilidad y compromiso por parte de aquellos que di-
rigen y enseñan en el Distrito.

MAYOR ATENCIÓN EN LOS PADRES

La Mesa y al Distrito ahora están poniendo mayor atención en
los padres. Nosotros escucharos sus preocupaciones, los in-
sitamos a que formen parte de committees para ayudar a di-
virnos a ser miembros de comité para todas las escuelas,
instituciones. Hemos pedido sugerencias a través de toda la
sistencias de padres para que respuesta a preguntas
y estarán efectivamente obtienen a las opiniones de los pa-
dres.

Yo agradezco a todos los padres que abordaron nuestro autobús
al Ayuntamiento de San Diego para ayudarnos a adquirir los
fondos de CDBG.

En conclusión, habrá posible una acción más poderosa y mayores
resultados después de la designación del Superintendente. For-
talezaremos nuestra afiliación después de la designación del Superintendente después de junio. Es-
el Distrito este dispuesto a mejorar. y compromete a
de Tito al manejo pulido de una efectiva comunicación educativa
atención al manejo pulido de una efectiva comunicación educativa.


[signature]
Bernard F. Armas, Jr.
Presidencia de la Mesa

B-6 SAN DIEGO TRIBUNE Thursday, June 14, 1990

Metro South news

Grand jury now focuses
on San Ysidro's Armas

By Julie Brossy
Tribune Staff Writer

New allegations have put the county grand jury back to work just when members were about to wrap up their investigation of the troubled San Ysidro school board.

The grand jury, a subject of months of speculation in this largely Spanish-speaking community bordering Tijuana, is now focusing on school board President Bernard Armas.

At issue is whether Armas acted improperly when he used district funds and personnel to write, print and distribute a letter that critics charge is blatantly political, said a grand jury member who declined to be further identified.

The lengthy investigation, which began last fall, has produced no criminal charges. Grand jury members, who have attended many school board meetings incognito in the course of their inquiry, say they will present a report of their findings sometime before the session ends later this month.

Armas, who along with another trustee is the target of a bitter recall campaign, last week reimbursed the school district for $233 worth of costs incurred in printing the one-page leaflet. The letter had been printed and distributed the previous week.

In a brief interview at the district office last week, Armas said that in writing the letter his intent was merely to send out the traditional school board president's message, as is done each year.

Once he realized that some people had misunderstood his intent, he said, he asked for the cost of the leaflet and paid that amount to the district.

But Toby Mange, another board member, charged that the amount

still did not account for the use of district personnel to translate, type and help distribute the leaflet.

Armas' critics turned the letter over to the grand jury.

In the letter, Armas says: "My promise to you when I asked for your vote 17 months ago was leadership that brings results, improved school facilities, greater accountability, and (being) more responsive to parents."

Critics say the letter was distributed in part through teachers and local schoolchildren.

The body of the letter lists, in very general terms, improvements that Armas thinks have been made in the district since his election.

The grand jury investigation, the first in the district, according to one longtime school official, began after three of the five school board members voted in September 1989 to accept the resignation of then-superintendent Gilbert Anzaldua.

Almost immediately, the same three voted to appoint Jose Melchor as interim superintendent, prompting charges from the two dissenting trustees that the whole sequence of events had been prearranged.

Since that vote, the board has been divided on most important issues, with trustees Armas, Juan Leyva and Norma Estrella pitted against

Mange and Ernestine Jones.

Armas and Leyva are now the subjects of a recall campaign by community members who contend they are incompetent and have become involved in conflicts of interest.

The controversy has been fueled by several additional disputes, including one centering on what many community members perceive as attempts by Armas to gather for his son, Bernie Armas Jr., the position of principal within the district.

Armas and Leyva say they are

merely trying to improve performance of the district long suffered from [illegible]. Necessarily, Armas and their actions have met the toes of people trenched interests.

10/24/90


DEAR DR. MONTALVO SUPERINTENDENT

SUBJECT:    PRESIDENT MESSAGE REFUND


IN THE GRANDJURY REPORT PERTAINING TO THE PRESIDENT'S MESSAGE
OF 1990 HAS NOT FOUND TO BE POLITICAL. I HEREBY REQUEST THAT A
REFUND ACCUMULATED INTEREST BE GIVEN ME. MY RECEIPT SHOWS
THAT ON JUNE 5TH 1990 I PAID $232.73.

I ALSO NEED TO INFORM YOU DR MONTALVO THAT NOT ONLY THE GRANDJURY'S
FINDINGS RULE IN MY FAVOR, BUT PROPOSITION 73 WAS REPEALED.
THEREFORE I EXPECT THIS MATTER TO BE COMPLETED BY OUR NEXT GOVERNING
BOARD MEETING.


DURING OUR TELEPHONE CONVERSATION OF LAST FRIDAY OCTOBER 19TH, YOU
STATED THAT I ASKED FOR THE ADDITIONAL AMOUNT THAT I SUPPOSEDLY
OWNED, WHICH WAS A BLUNDER ON YOUR PART I DID NOT ASKED FOR YOU TO
SEND ME AN ESTIMATED EXPENSE LIST.

I DONT APPRECIATE THIS KIND OF HARASSMENT.          (please see attached copy from
                                                     (Dr. Montalvo)

                                                     SINCERELY,

cc: Ken Clark
    Richard Currier                                  GOVERNING BOARD PRESIDENT
    GrandJury
    Governing Board

sections.conclusion:
, more forceful action and greater results will be possible following the appointment of the permanent Superintendent. This Board will take action in late June. This District is ready for improvement, commitment and attention to the smooth running of an effective educational community.

[Signature]
Bernard F. Armas, Sr.
Board President

El documento es un mensaje del presidente de la Mesa Directiva del Distrito Escolar de San Ysidro, emitido el 29 de mayo de 1990. El presidente menciona su promesa hecha hace 17 meses, cuando los padres votaron por él, y destaca los siguientes puntos:

1. **Liderazgo que produzca resultados**: Desde que se convirtió en miembro de la Mesa Directiva, ha existido en el Distrito una vigorosa y activa dirección. Se han hecho preguntas sobre resultados de pruebas, seguridad en la escuela, descripción de estudiantes, aprendizaje en el salón de clases y transferencias de estudiantes entre distritos. Además, se ha hecho a la gente más responsable y se ha elevado el nivel de compromiso.

2. **Mejores instalaciones escolares**: La mayor parte de nuestras escuelas están a toda su capacidad o excedidas. Se están haciendo pláticas con la Ciudad y los urbanizadores de Otay Mesa sobre instalaciones adicionales, además de procurar el hermosamiento y limpieza de todos nuestros planteles. Tal vez para la próxima primavera veremos más verde y flores alrededor de nuestras escuelas. Necesitamos enorgullecernos más de nuestras instalaciones escolares.

3. **Mayor responsabilidad**: Debido al interés de la Mesa y de los padres, los administradores y los maestros están siendo evaluados cuidadosamente, y se han establecido metas y objetivos. Algunos resultados de pruebas no están aún disponibles, pero serán un indicio de la responsabilidad y compromiso por parte de aquellos que dirigen y enseñan en el Distrito.

4. **Mayor atención en los padres**: La Mesa y el Distrito ahora están poniendo mayor atención en los padres. Nosotros escuchamos sus preocupaciones, los invitamos a ser miembros de comités para solucionar problemas, instalamos "cajas para sugerencias" en todas las escuelas, sostengamos sesiones de padres para dar respuesta a sus preguntas y estamos efectivamente abiertas a las opiniones de los padres.

El presidente agradece a todos los padres que abordaron nuestro autobús al Ayuntamiento de San Diego para ayudarnos a adquirir los fondos de CDBG.

En conclusión, será posible una acción más poderosa y mayores resultados después de la designación del Superintendente Permanente. Esta Mesa tomará una decisión a fines de junio. Este Distrito está dispuesto a mejorar, a comprometerse y poner atención al manejo pulido de una efectiva comunidad educativa.

Firma: Bernard F. Armas, Sr.
Presidente de la Mesa

**Grand Jury Now Focuses on San Ysidro's Armas**

By Julie Brossy  
Tribune Staff Writer

New allegations have put the county grand jury back to work just when members were about to wrap up their investigation of the troubled San Ysidro school board.

The grand jury inquiry, a subject of months of speculation in this largely Spanish-speaking community bordering Tijuana, is now focusing on school board President Bernard Armas.

At issue is whether Armas acted improperly when he used district funds and personnel to write, print and distribute a letter that critics charge is blatantly political, said a grand jury member who declined to be further identified.

The lengthy investigation, which began last fall, has produced no criminal charges. Grand jury members, who have attended many school board meetings incognito in the course of their inquiry, say they will present a report of their findings sometime before the session ends later this month.

Armas, who along with another trustee is the target of a bitter recall campaign, last week reimbursed the school district for $233 worth of costs incurred in printing the one-page leaflet. The letter had been printed and distributed the previous week.

In a brief interview at the district office last week, Armas said that in writing the letter his intent was merely to send out the traditional school board president's message, as is done each year.

Once he realized that some people had misunderstood his intent, he said, he asked for the cost of the leaflet and paid that amount to the district.

But Toby Mange, another board member, charged that the amount still did not account for the use of district personnel to translate, type and help distribute the leaflet.

Armas' critics turned the letter over to the grand jury.

In the letter, Armas says: "My promise to you when I asked for your vote 17 months ago was leadership that brings results, improved school facilities, greater accountability, and (being) more responsive to parents."

Critics say the letter was distributed in part through teachers and local schoolchildren.

The body of the letter lists, in very general terms, improvements that Armas thinks have been made in the district since his election.

The grand jury investigation, the first in the district, according to one longtime school official, began after three of the five school board members voted in September 1989 to accept the resignation of then-Superintendent Gilbert Anzaldua.

Almost immediately, the same three voted to appoint Jose Melchor as interim superintendent, prompting charges from the two dissenting trustees that the whole sequence of events had been prearranged.

Since that vote, the board has been divided on most important issues, with trustees Armas, Juan Leyva and Norma Estrella pitted against Mange and Ernestine Jones.

Armas and Leyva are now the subjects of a recall campaign by community members who contend they are incompetent and have become involved in conflicts of interest.

The controversy has been fueled by several additional disputes, including one centering on what many community members perceive as attempts by Armas to garner for his son, Bernie Armas Jr., the position of principal within the district.

Armas and Leyva say they are merely trying to improve performance of the district long suffered from low morale. Necessarily, Armas and Leyva say their actions have meant stepping on the toes of people with entrenched interests.

10/24/90

DEAR DR. MONTALVO SUPERINTENDENT

SUBJECT: PRESIDENT MESSAGE REFUND

IN THE GRANDJURY REPORT PERTAINING TO THE PRESIDENT'S MESSAGE OF 1990 WAS NOT FOUND TO BE POLITICAL. I HEREBY REQUEST THAT A REFUND PLUS ACCUMULATED INTEREST BE GIVEN ME. MY RECEIPT SHOWS THAT ON JUNE 5TH 1990 I PAID $232.73.

I ALSO NEED TO INFORM YOU DR MONTALVO THAT NOT ONLY THE GRANDJURY'S FINDINGS RULE IN MY FAVOR, BUT PROPOSITION 73 WAS REPEALED. THEREFORE I EXPECT THIS MATTER TO BE COMPLETED BY OUR NEXT GOVERNING BOARD MEETING.

DURING OUR TELEPHONE CONVERSATION OF LAST FRIDAY OCTOBER 19TH, YOU STATED THAT I ASKED FOR THE ADDITIONAL AMOUNT THAT I SUPPOSEDLY OWNED, WHICH WAS A BLUNTER ON YOUR PART I DID NOT ASKED FOR YOU TO SEND ME AN ESTIMATED EXPENSE LIST,

I DON'T APPRECIATE THIS KIND OF HARASSMENT.

(please see attached copy from (Dr. Montalvo))

cc: Ken Clark
    Richard Currier
    Grand Jury
    Governing Board

This request for a written opinion concerns an unresolved controversy in the District which began on May 29, 1990, and continues to the present. On May 29, 1990, Board President Bernardo F. Armas, Sr., directed that a written message be prepared, copied and delivered to all students in the District. A copy of that written message is the first enclosure with this letter. The written message also was translated into Spanish and delivered to students in the District. A copy of the translated message is the second enclosure with this letter. There are six schools in the District and approximately 3,700 students. Two of the Board members objected to the written message, but it was processed at the direction of Mr. Armas. After the written message from Mr. Armas was delivered to all students in the District, the then current Interim Superintendent asked the attorneys for the District for a legal opinion. Attorneys for the District advised the Interim Superintendent orally and later in writing that the use of public funds on May 29, 1990, violated Government Code section 89001 (as amended by Proposition 73 in 1988) and regulations adopted by the Fair Political Practices Commission. 4350 Otay Mesa Road, San Ysidro, CA 92073-1685 (619) 428-4476 Executive Director Fair Political Practices Commission November 15, 1990 Page 2 On June 5, 1990, Mr. Armas delivered a personal check to the District for $232.73 which reimbursed the District for the cost of duplicating his written message. Shortly thereafter the Grand Jury in San Diego County announced it was investigating the mat- ter and other issues involving the District. The third enclosure with this letter is a copy of a newspaper article on the con- troversy dated June 14, 1990. On July 1, 1990, I started my employment with the District as the Superintendent. On October 16, 1990, the Grand Jury issued a report noting on page 3 in paragraph eight that the Dis- trict Attorney found no criminal conduct involving newsletters in 1990 and earlier years. A copy of that report is the fourth enclosure with this letter. On October 19, 1990, I sent Mr. Armas a letter requesting an additional $153.00, because the costs of his written message in- cluded more than copying costs. A copy of that letter is the fifth enclosure with this letter. On October 24, 1990, Mr. Armas sent me a letter requesting a refund of the $232.73. A copy of his letter is the sixth and final enclosure with this letter. Mr. Armas now argues that the report of the Grand Jury "rule in his favor" and that Proposition 73 was repealed.
